skill.name: apscheduler
skill.description: Guidance for building Python background jobs with APScheduler triggers, persistence, async execution, and distributed workers.
skill.summary: Build recurring, one-off, and distributed Python jobs with APScheduler.
skill.author: bossjones

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, database, weak-crypto, and obfuscation signals, but review found they are documentation false positives. The scanned skill contains markdown examples for APScheduler and an embedded evaluation JSON; no executable implementation, credential access, prompt injection, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to markdown code fences and sample code blocks. They document APScheduler usage and do not execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited SKILL.md lines are markdown code block delimiters or documentation examples. The evaluation.json line is a JSON string describing that same false positive, not executable code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Placeholder Network URL in Documentation
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are example endpoint strings inside documentation. No runtime code performs an outbound request from the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SKILL.md location uses api.example.com inside a sample async job. The evaluation.json location is explanatory text about the placeholder URL.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Persistence Examples Treated as Sensitive File Access
low_findings.description: The SQLite database finding is a sample connection string showing APScheduler persistence. It does not read, write, or exfiltrate a real database file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited SKILL.md line is a documented SQLite configuration example. The evaluation.json line is a prior explanation, not code that accesses the filesystem.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Heuristic Has No Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings point to scheduler descriptions and ordinary data-processing examples. No cryptographic algorithm usage appears in the scanned skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations contain description text, function parameters, S3 example strings, or explanatory JSON text. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak crypto use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Critical Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined documentation examples for code blocks, URLs, and database configuration. Review found no executable payload, credential access, or obfuscation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious combination is explained by documentation context and an embedded prior audit record. No evidence found of hidden instructions, encoded payloads, or credential collection.

content.value_statement: Scheduling Python jobs reliably is hard when triggers, persistence, and async execution vary. This skill guides APScheduler setup for recurring, one-off, and distributed jobs.
actual_capabilities: Explains basic Scheduler and AsyncScheduler setup patterns.
Shows interval, cron, date, and calendar interval triggers.
Covers persistent schedules with SQLAlchemy-backed data stores.
Describes distributed scheduler and worker roles with event brokers.
Documents job result retrieval, event subscriptions, and schedule management.
Lists optional dependencies for PostgreSQL, SQLite, Redis, MongoDB, and MQTT.
limitations: Provides guidance and examples, not a runnable scheduler service.
Does not validate a project-specific APScheduler version or dependency set.
Uses placeholder connection strings that must be replaced before production use.
Does not include monitoring dashboards, retry policies, or deployment automation.

use_cases.title: Schedule recurring application tasks
use_cases.description: Create daily reports, cleanup jobs, and periodic maintenance tasks inside Python services.

use_cases.title: Run async work beside FastAPI
use_cases.description: Use AsyncScheduler lifecycle patterns for jobs that run alongside an async web application.

use_cases.title: Plan persistent distributed scheduling
use_cases.description: Design scheduler and worker roles with shared storage and event brokers for production jobs.

prompt_templates.title: Create a simple interval job
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one recurring task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the APScheduler skill to help me schedule a Python cleanup function every 10 minutes.

prompt_templates.title: Add cron scheduling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the APScheduler skill to design a weekday 9 AM report job with clear trigger settings.

prompt_templates.title: Make schedules persistent
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the APScheduler skill to convert an in-memory scheduler plan to PostgreSQL persistence.

prompt_templates.title: Design distributed workers
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the APScheduler skill to outline scheduler and worker nodes with shared storage and an event broker.

output_examples.input: I need a daily report job that runs at 9 AM on weekdays.
output: Recommended trigger: a cron schedule for Monday through Friday at 09:00.
Recommended job shape: keep report generation separate from email delivery error handling.
Production note: use a persistent data store so the schedule survives restarts.

output_examples.input: My FastAPI app needs a background cleanup task every hour.
output: Recommended scheduler: AsyncScheduler started during the application lifespan.
Recommended trigger: an hourly interval trigger with a clear schedule identifier.
Operational note: subscribe to job events so failed cleanup runs are visible.

output_examples.input: We need multiple workers to execute scheduled jobs.
output: Recommended architecture: separate scheduler nodes from worker nodes.
Shared components: use a durable data store and an event broker.
Deployment note: configure concurrency limits before increasing worker count.

best_practices: Use persistent data stores for production schedules that must survive restarts.
Set misfire grace time, coalescing, and concurrency limits for predictable recovery.
Subscribe to scheduler events so failures and missed runs become observable.
anti_patterns: Do not store secrets directly in schedules, job arguments, or connection examples.
Do not run very short intervals without concurrency and coalescing controls.
Do not rely on in-memory schedules for production workflows that require durability.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design Python background jobs using APScheduler triggers, schedulers, persistence, and worker roles.

faq.question: Does this skill execute scheduled jobs by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and examples that guide implementation in your Python application.

faq.question: Can it help with FastAPI applications?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes AsyncScheduler patterns for running jobs during a FastAPI application lifespan.

faq.question: How should production schedules be stored?
faq.answer: Use a persistent data store such as SQLAlchemy-backed PostgreSQL or SQLite, based on your deployment needs.

faq.question: Can APScheduler replace a full task queue?
faq.answer: It can handle scheduling and job execution, but complex queues may still need dedicated systems.

faq.question: Are the sample URLs and database strings production ready?
faq.answer: No. They are placeholders and must be replaced with secure project-specific configuration.
